content_markdown:
The pressure–volume ($P$–$V$) graph shows an adiabatic compression of a fixed mass of an ideal gas.

Which of the following correctly describes what happens to the temperature and the internal energy of the gas during the compression?

diagram_metadata.description: A P-V graph showing a curve moving from a lower volume and higher pressure to a higher volume and lower pressure, but the arrow indicates the process is moving from right to left (compression).

options.label: B
options.text: Temperature: increases; Internal energy: no change

options.label: C
options.text: Temperature: decreases; Internal energy: no change

vibe_explanation: In an adiabatic compression, no heat is exchanged ($Q = 0$). According to the first law of thermodynamics, $\Delta U = Q - W$. Since the gas is being compressed, work is done on the gas ($W$ is negative), making $\Delta U$ positive. Therefore, the internal energy increases. For an ideal gas, internal energy is directly proportional to absolute temperature, so the temperature must also increase.
